text.title: Test Data Patterns: Builder and Object Mother
text.summary: Test Data Builder provides a fluent API for constructing objects with sensible defaults; Object Mother provides pre-named scenarios. Combine them: Object Mother returns a Builder for flexible customization.

rule.statement: Create a Builder class per domain entity that initializes all fields with valid defaults; each with-* method returns `this` for chaining; build() returns a deep copy of the accumulated state.
rule.rationale: Tests should specify only the properties relevant to the scenario under test. Builder defaults prevent test code from depending on irrelevant fields, making tests resilient to schema changes.

rule.statement: Create an Object Mother with static factory methods for common test scenarios; each method returns a configured Builder so callers can further customize before calling build().
rule.rationale: Object Mother eliminates copy-paste of common setup across test files. Returning a Builder (not a built object) gives callers the flexibility to override specific fields without duplicating the whole preset.

rule.statement: Each test must create its own test data from scratch; never share mutable fixture objects across tests via module-level variables.
rule.rationale: Shared mutable fixtures create test-order dependencies. When one test mutates a shared object, subsequent tests may pass or fail depending on execution order — a flaky test root cause.

antipattern.description: Module-level mutable fixture objects shared across tests.
antipattern.reason: Any mutation by one test leaves the fixture in unexpected state for the next test, causing intermittent failures.
